name: Marbury v. Madison
holding: The Supreme Court has the power of judicial review — the authority to declare acts of Congress unconstitutional and void. An executive order of mandamus may issue against an officer to compel a non-discretionary, ministerial duty. Congress cannot expand the Supreme Court's original jurisdiction beyond what Article III provides.
significance: The foundational case of American constitutional law, establishing the judiciary as the ultimate interpreter of the Constitution. Marshall's strategic brilliance — finding the Court lacked jurisdiction while simultaneously announcing the sweeping power of judicial review — set the course for over two centuries of constitutional adjudication.
keyQuote: It is emphatically the province and duty of the judicial department to say what the law is.

name: McCulloch v. Maryland
holding: Congress has the power to create a national bank under the Necessary and Proper Clause, which permits Congress to employ means reasonably adapted to executing its enumerated powers. A state may not tax a federal instrumentality because the power to tax is the power to destroy.
significance: Established that the Necessary and Proper Clause augments Congress's enumerated powers to include incidental powers necessary to their execution, and that 'necessary' means useful or convenient, not indispensable. The principle that implied powers flow from enumerated powers recurs throughout separation of powers doctrine.
keyQuote: Let the end be legitimate, let it be within the scope of the constitution, and all means which are appropriate, which are plainly adapted to that end, which are not prohibited, but consist with the letter and spirit of the constitution, are constitutional.

name: Wickard v. Filburn
holding: Congress may regulate homegrown wheat not intended for interstate commerce because, when aggregated with all similar activity nationwide, it substantially affects interstate commerce. The aggregation principle allows Congress to reach purely intrastate activity if the cumulative effect on commerce is substantial.
significance: Represents the high point of Commerce Clause power and established the aggregation principle that makes it far easier to show a substantial relation to interstate commerce. Between Wickard and Lopez (1995), Congress never lost a Commerce Clause case.

name: Youngstown Sheet & Tube Co. v. Sawyer
holding: The President's seizure of steel mills during the Korean War was not authorized by any statute or constitutional provision. Justice Jackson's concurrence established the three-category framework: presidential power is at its zenith when acting with congressional authorization (Category 1), in a zone of twilight when Congress is silent (Category 2), and at its lowest ebb when acting contrary to Congress's will (Category 3).
significance: The foundational case for separation of powers analysis. Jackson's three-category framework is the starting point for any question involving a clash between presidential and congressional power. Frankfurter's concurrence introduced the concept of historical practice as constitutional gloss.
keyQuote: The framers suspected that emergency powers would tend to kindle emergencies.

name: Baker v. Carr
holding: An equal protection challenge to legislative malapportionment is justiciable and not barred by the political question doctrine. The Court identified six factors for determining whether a case presents a political question, with the first two — textual commitment to a coordinate branch and lack of judicially manageable standards — being the most important.
significance: The classic case establishing the framework for the political question doctrine and opening the door to judicial review of redistricting under the Equal Protection Clause. Led directly to Reynolds v. Sims and the one-person-one-vote principle.

name: Reynolds v. Sims
holding: The Equal Protection Clause requires legislative districts to contain roughly equal populations — the one person, one vote principle. Malapportioned districts violate the Fourteenth Amendment.
significance: Established the fundamental constitutional requirement that all legislative districts must be drawn with roughly equal populations, generating the redistricting obligation after each census and setting the stage for modern gerrymandering disputes.

name: United States v. Nixon
holding: The President has a constitutionally based but not absolute privilege to keep communications confidential. When the privilege is weighed against the demands of criminal justice, the balance favors the fair administration of criminal justice. Nixon was required to produce subpoenaed tapes and papers.
significance: Established that executive privilege is implicit in the Constitution, deriving from the supremacy of each branch within its own duties, but is subject to a balancing test. Explicit presidential powers receive categorical protection; implicit powers like executive privilege are subject to interest balancing.
keyQuote: Nowhere in the Constitution is there any explicit reference to a privilege of confidentiality, yet to the extent this interest relates to the effective discharge of a President's powers, it is constitutionally based.

name: Nixon v. Fitzgerald
holding: A former President has absolute immunity from civil damages suits for official acts taken while in office. The immunity attaches to the President's function, not the individual.
significance: First case to recognize absolute presidential immunity, establishing a key precedent for Trump v. United States. The distinction between official and unofficial acts became central to the immunity framework.

name: INS v. Chadha
holding: The legislative veto is unconstitutional. When Congress acts in a way that is legislative in purpose and effect — altering the legal rights, duties, and relations of persons outside the legislative branch — it must act through bicameralism and presentment. A one-house or two-house veto that bypasses either requirement is invalid.
significance: A landmark formalist separation of powers decision that eliminated Congress's most powerful tool for controlling administrative agencies. Justice White's functionalist dissent argued that if the Court allows delegation, it should also allow Congress to retain a veto — a tension that persists in administrative law.

name: A.L.A. Schechter Poultry Corp. v. United States
holding: The National Industrial Recovery Act's delegation of authority to create industry codes violated the non-delegation doctrine because Congress gave no intelligible principle to guide executive discretion. The Act also exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power.
significance: One of only two statutes ever struck down on non-delegation grounds. Decided on 'Black Monday' in 1935, it represents the high-water mark of judicial resistance to FDR's New Deal. Though never followed again, the case has never been overruled and could return if the doctrine is reinvigorated.

name: Panama Refining Co. v. Ryan
holding: The 'hot oil' provision of the National Industrial Recovery Act was an unconstitutional delegation of legislative power because Congress provided no guidance to the executive branch in exercising the delegated authority.
significance: Along with Schechter Poultry, one of only two cases striking down a statute on non-delegation grounds. Together they represent the brief period in 1935 when the doctrine had real teeth — a period that conservative justices now seek to revive.

name: Gundy v. United States
holding: A plurality upheld SORNA's delegation to the Attorney General to apply registration requirements to pre-Act offenders. The Court found an intelligible principle in the statutory scheme, but the decision revealed significant appetite among conservative justices to reinvigorate the non-delegation doctrine.
significance: Signals a potential future tightening of the non-delegation doctrine. Justice Gorsuch's dissent argued Congress cannot delegate legislative power and criticized the intelligible principle test as toothless, reflecting the broader movement to expand presidential power by forcing Congress to legislate directly.
keyQuote: Legislators might seek to take credit for addressing a social problem by sending it to the executive for resolution, while at the same time blaming the executive for the problems that attend whatever measures he chooses to pursue.

name: Buckley v. Valeo
holding: Individuals who exercise significant governmental authority — including enforcement power, regulation-making, and determining eligibility for funds — are 'officers of the United States' who must be appointed consistent with the Appointments Clause. Congress may not appoint executive officers.
significance: Established the threshold test for who qualifies as an 'officer of the United States' under the Appointments Clause, a distinction that determines whether the Appointments Clause's requirements apply. The significant governmental authority standard remains the operative test.

name: Lucia v. SEC
holding: Administrative law judges at the SEC who make final decisions are officers of the United States and must be appointed in accordance with the Appointments Clause. The power to make final decisions — decisions that can but need not be reviewed — elevates an ALJ to officer status.
significance: Extended the Buckley v. Valeo framework to adjudicatory officers within agencies, holding that ALJs who render decisions that can become final are officers, not mere employees. Has significant implications for the structure of administrative adjudication.

name: United States v. Arthrex, Inc.
holding: Patent judges at the PTAB who had administrative supervision but no decisional review from a superior were principal officers unconstitutionally appointed. To be an inferior officer, one must be supervised both administratively and with respect to decisions.
significance: Refined the distinction between principal and inferior officers by requiring that inferior officers be supervised in both administrative and decisional capacities. Without decisional review, an officer is principal and must be appointed by the President with Senate confirmation.

name: Myers v. United States
holding: Congress cannot reserve to itself the right to participate in the removal of an executive officer. The statute requiring Senate consent for removing a postmaster was unconstitutional.
significance: Established the foundational principle that Congress may not involve itself in the removal of executive officers — a principle the executive branch relies on in every removal power dispute. Distinguished from later cases addressing whether Congress can limit the President's own removal power.

name: Humphrey's Executor v. United States
holding: Congress may limit the President's removal power over heads of multi-member commissions that exercise quasi-legislative and quasi-judicial functions. The FTC's for-cause removal protection was constitutional.
significance: The foundation of the independent agency model, permitting Congress to insulate certain officers from at-will presidential removal. Currently under severe threat — the Court signaled in Trump v. Wilcox that it will likely overrule this precedent.

name: Morrison v. Olson
holding: Congress may restrict the President's removal of an inferior officer so long as the restriction does not 'unduly trammel' or 'impermissibly burden' the President's constitutional obligations. The independent counsel statute's for-cause removal protection was upheld 8-1.
significance: Replaced Humphrey's Executor's rigid categories with a functionalist balancing test for removal restrictions. Justice Scalia's famous dissent — arguing executive power is indivisible and the majority's test is 'no rule at all' — has now become the ascendant view at the Court.
keyQuote: This wolf comes as a wolf.

name: Seila Law LLC v. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau
holding: An independent agency headed by a single director who is insulated from presidential at-will removal violates the separation of powers. Congress cannot restrict the President's removal of a single agency head.
significance: Extended the removal power doctrine by distinguishing single-headed agencies from multi-member commissions. Signaled the Court's trajectory toward greater presidential control over the executive branch and the potential demise of Humphrey's Executor.

name: Collins v. Yellen
holding: The Federal Housing Finance Agency's structure — a single director removable only for cause — violated the separation of powers under Seila Law. The appropriate remedy was to sever the removal restriction rather than invalidate the agency's actions.
significance: Confirmed that Seila Law's prohibition on insulating single agency heads from presidential removal applies broadly, and established severability as the default remedy for unconstitutional removal restrictions.

name: Trump v. Wilcox
holding: On the emergency docket, the Court stayed lower court injunctions against the President's removal of NLRB and MSPB board members, stating that the President 'may remove without cause executive officers who exercise [executive] power on his behalf, subject to narrow exceptions recognized by our precedents.'
significance: Signals the near-certain overruling of Humphrey's Executor and the end of the independent agency model as traditionally understood. The most significant recent development in the trend toward expanding presidential removal power.
keyQuote: Because the Constitution vests the executive power in the President, he may remove without cause executive officers who exercise that power on his behalf, subject to narrow exceptions recognized by our precedents.

name: Trump v. United States
holding: The President has absolute immunity for actions involving core presidential powers, at least presumptive immunity for actions within the outer perimeter of official responsibilities (shared or twilight-zone powers), and no immunity for unofficial acts. Courts may not inquire into the President's motives, and evidence of official acts cannot be used to prosecute unofficial acts.
significance: The most expansive presidential immunity decision in history. Justice Sotomayor's dissent argued the majority ignores the rule-of-law side of the balance and that the space for truly 'unofficial' acts may be vanishingly small. A maximalist opinion that fundamentally reshaped the law of presidential accountability.
keyQuote: The President must be free to act with energy, vigor, and decisiveness.

name: Clinton v. Jones
holding: A sitting President has no immunity from civil damages suits for unofficial acts — including conduct that occurred before the President took office. The Constitution does not require federal courts to stay or dismiss private civil damages actions against a sitting President.
significance: Established that presidential immunity does not extend to unofficial or pre-presidential conduct in civil cases. Provides the contrast to Nixon v. Fitzgerald's absolute immunity for official acts and forms part of the case law matrix leading to Trump v. United States.

name: United States v. Lopez
holding: The Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power. Possessing a gun near a school is not economic activity, cannot be aggregated, is not an essential part of a larger economic regulatory scheme, and the causal chain linking it to interstate commerce requires impermissible piling of inference upon inference.
significance: The first case in over fifty years to strike down a federal statute under the Commerce Clause, establishing the modern three-category framework and signaling that Congress's commerce power has real limits. Ended the post-1937 era of effectively unlimited Commerce Clause authority.

name: United States v. Morrison
holding: The Violence Against Women Act's civil remedy exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power despite extensive congressional findings linking gender-motivated violence to interstate commerce. The activity was not economic, the causal chain was too attenuated, and upholding it would allow federal intrusion into the states' traditional police power over violent crime.
significance: Confirmed that Lopez was not an anomaly and that Congress's Commerce Clause power has meaningful limits. Reinforced the distinction between economic and non-economic activity and the prohibition on piling inference upon inference.

name: Gonzales v. Raich
holding: Congress may regulate purely intrastate, non-commercial activity — growing cannabis for personal medical use — if failure to regulate that class of activity would undercut the regulation of the interstate market in that commodity. The Controlled Substances Act's application to homegrown cannabis was constitutional under the 'essential part of a larger regulation' theory.
significance: Revived Wickard v. Filburn and confirmed it remains good law despite Lopez and Morrison. Established that non-economic activity can be reached under the Commerce Clause if it is an essential part of a larger economic regulatory scheme. The 'essential part' theory does not require total scheme collapse — just a 'pretty big hole.'

name: National Federation of Independent Business v. Sebelius
holding: The individual mandate exceeded Congress's Commerce Clause power because Congress cannot compel individuals to enter commerce — the power to regulate presupposes the existence of commercial activity. However, the mandate was sustained as a valid exercise of the taxing power. The Medicaid expansion's threat to withhold all Medicaid funding was unconstitutionally coercive under the Spending Clause.
significance: Established the activity/inactivity distinction as a threshold Commerce Clause limit, articulated the tax-vs-penalty framework, and set benchmarks for Spending Clause coercion analysis. A landmark case that draws together three distinct congressional powers in one opinion.

name: South Dakota v. Dole
holding: Congress may condition 5% of federal highway funds on states raising the drinking age to 21. The condition was germane to highway safety and the amount was not so great as to be coercive — the state retained a real choice to decline the funds.
significance: Established the Spending Clause analytical framework for conditional funding: conditions must be germane to the program, and the amount must not be so great as to be coercive. Set the lower benchmark (5% of highway funds is not coercive) that NFIB v. Sebelius later contrasted with 10% of entire state budgets.

name: New York v. United States
holding: Congress cannot commandeer state legislatures by ordering them to adopt regulations. A statute requiring states to either regulate nuclear waste or take title to it presented a choice between two unconstitutional options and violated the Tenth Amendment.
significance: The foundational anti-commandeering case establishing that the federal government cannot order state legislatures to enact or administer a federal regulatory program. The underlying rationale is democratic accountability — citizens must be able to determine whether to blame the state or federal government.

name: Printz v. United States
holding: Congress cannot commandeer state executive officers to administer or enforce a federal regulatory program. The Brady Bill's requirement that state law enforcement officers conduct background checks in an interim period violated the Tenth Amendment.
significance: Extended New York v. United States from state legislatures to state executive officials, reinforcing that the federal government cannot conscript state officers to carry out federal law. The principle explains why the federal government cannot order state police to arrest people for federal cannabis violations.

name: Murphy v. NCAA
holding: Congress cannot prohibit states from authorizing activity (sports gambling). Telling a state 'you may not make this legal' is functionally the same as telling it 'you must make this illegal,' which violates the anti-commandeering doctrine of the Tenth Amendment.
significance: Extended the anti-commandeering doctrine to its logical conclusion: Congress cannot command states to keep something illegal any more than it can command them to make something illegal. Directly enabled the legalization of sports betting nationwide and illustrates the cannabis federalism dynamic.

name: United States v. Curtiss-Wright Export Corp.
holding: Congress's delegation to the President of the power to prohibit arms sales upon a presidential finding that such a ban would contribute to peace was constitutional. The Court upheld the delegation and added extensive dicta about the President as the 'sole organ' of foreign relations with powers that do not require congressional authorization.
significance: The foundational case for broad presidential foreign affairs power and a Youngstown Category 1 case. The 'sole organ' dicta is cited whenever presidential foreign affairs authority is at issue, though it is mostly dicta. Distinguished foreign affairs delegation from domestic delegation.
keyQuote: The President alone has the power to speak or listen as a representative of the nation. He makes treaties with the advice and consent of the Senate; but he alone negotiates.

name: Dames & Moore v. Regan
holding: The President's suspension of claims by U.S. plaintiffs against Iranian defendants as part of the Iran hostage crisis resolution was constitutional. Although no statute directly authorized the suspension, the 'general tenor' of congressional legislation in the area indicated congressional acceptance of a broad scope for executive action — a Youngstown Category 1.5 situation.
significance: Reconceived the Youngstown framework as a spectrum rather than three rigid categories. Introduced the concept that the 'general tenor' of statutes can support presidential action even without specific authorization. The President's long, unbroken history of settling claims through executive agreements supported the holding.

name: Medellin v. Texas
holding: The President cannot enforce a non-self-executing treaty domestically through an executive memorandum without implementing legislation from Congress. The Vienna Convention's consular notification requirement was non-self-executing, and the President's attempt to direct state courts to comply was an unprecedented action lacking any history of congressional acquiescence. This was Youngstown Category 3, and the President lost.
significance: Established the critical distinction between self-executing and non-self-executing treaties. Demonstrates that presidential foreign affairs power, while strong, has limits — the broad dicta of Curtis-Wright does not authorize the President to do whatever he wants in foreign affairs.

name: Zivotofsky v. Kerry
holding: The power to recognize foreign governments is an exclusive presidential power, inferred from the power to receive ambassadors and the treaty power. Even at Youngstown Category 3 — acting contrary to Congress's expressed will — the President prevails because recognition is an exclusive, preclusive presidential power that Congress cannot restrict.
significance: The critical case where the President wins in Youngstown Category 3. For exam and analysis purposes, if the President faces a Category 3 scenario, Zivotofsky is the precedent — but the power must be truly exclusive and preclusive. Demonstrates that one voice must speak for the nation on recognition.

name: Rucho v. Common Cause
holding: Political gerrymandering claims are nonjusticiable political questions. Once some degree of political gerrymandering is conceded as inevitable and permissible, there are no judicially manageable standards for determining when it has gone 'too far.'
significance: The landmark holding removing federal courts from policing partisan gerrymandering. Justice Kagan's dissent proposed a three-part test (political purpose, substantial dilution, no nonpartisan justification) that the majority rejected. Racial gerrymandering remains justiciable but is hard to prove.

name: Bush v. Gore
holding: The use of inconsistent recount standards across Florida counties in the 2000 presidential election violated the Equal Protection Clause. The Court effectively decided the presidential election by halting the recount.
significance: Powerfully illustrates the 'unprincipled' nature of the political question doctrine's application — the Court was willing to decide arguably the most political question of all (a presidential election) despite the doctrine it uses to avoid far less politically charged questions. Justice Breyer dissented, arguing this was a political question.
